refined_fr.refined_fr: Enable measurement and reporting of material throughput in volumetric or mass units per unit time.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the system's capability to measure/report throughput metrics, and (2) the achievement of a customer-defined target. The latter is a success criterion dependent on operational execution, not a system requirement. The refined FR isolates the system's functional obligation—providing visibility into throughput—which is solution-neutral and testable independent of whether a specific productivity target is met. The target itself belongs in acceptance criteria or KPI definitions, not in the FR.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that the NGHE solution captures and reports material throughput (m³/h or ton/h) during representative operations. Verify data accuracy against independent measurement. Confirm reporting is accessible to Site Manager and relevant stakeholders in real time or near-real time.
sub_frs: Acquire material throughput data (volume or mass per time interval) during equipment operation.
Calculate and aggregate throughput metrics from acquired data.
Report throughput metrics to authorized stakeholders in human-readable format.

candidate_dps: DP1: Continuous volumetric flow measurement via inline sensor with real-time signal conditioning and local data buffering
DP2: Periodic mass-based sampling with calibrated weighing and extrapolation to throughput rate over evaluation window
DP3: Hybrid dual-path measurement (volumetric + mass) with cross-validation logic to detect sensor drift or anomalies
DP4: Indirect throughput inference from process parameters (e.g., pump speed, material density, operational time) with lookup tables or empirical models
DP5: Decoupled measurement pipeline: acquire raw sensor data → normalize to standard conditions → apply acceptance criteria logic → report results
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP5: Decoupled measurement pipeline with pluggable sensor abstraction, normalization layer, and acceptance criteria evaluation

potential_sources: Tight coupling between sensor acquisition and reporting logic (mitigated by abstraction layer)
Dependency on external calibration data or environmental correction factors (managed via explicit normalization module)
Acceptance criteria evaluation tightly bound to measurement method (decoupled via criteria-agnostic evaluation module)
coupling_check.notes: DP5 achieves low coupling by separating concerns: (1) sensor data acquisition (pluggable, method-agnostic), (2) unit conversion and environmental normalization (reusable across methods), (3) acceptance criteria evaluation (independent of measurement technology). This allows volumetric or mass methods to be swapped without redesign of reporting or validation logic. Cross-validation (DP3) can be layered as an optional enhancement without breaking the core pipeline.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Sensor data quality and calibration are maintained within acceptable tolerances during evaluation period. Risk: Drift or failure undetected → reported throughput exceeds actual. Mitigation: Implement anomaly detection and periodic recalibration checkpoints.

Assumption: Environmental conditions (temperature, pressure, material density) remain stable or are measurable. Risk: Uncompensated variation → systematic bias in throughput calculation. Mitigation: Explicit normalization module with environmental sensor inputs.

Assumption: Evaluation period is long enough to average transient fluctuations and establish statistical confidence. Risk: Short windows or high variability → acceptance criteria fail spuriously. Mitigation: Define minimum evaluation duration and acceptable variance bounds in acceptance criteria.

Assumption: Worksite safety plan does not prohibit sensor placement or data collection methods. Risk: Preferred measurement location inaccessible or unsafe. Mitigation: Early coordination with safety review; DP5 flexibility allows alternative sensor positions if needed.

Assumption: Reported productivity is defined consistently (e.g., net material, accounting for spillage/loss). Risk: Ambiguity in what counts toward throughput → acceptance criteria interpretation disputes. Mitigation: Clarify definition in acceptance criteria document before DP realization.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR to explicitly state: (1) whether throughput includes or excludes material losses (spillage, evaporation, etc.); (2) required measurement accuracy (e.g., ±5% of target); (3) environmental conditions under which normalization applies; (4) minimum evaluation period and statistical confidence level for acceptance. This will reduce ambiguity in DP5 realization and IM boundary definition (e.g., whether loss-correction logic belongs in the normalization module or upstream process control).

im_boundary.name: Safety Services
im_boundary.scope: Centralized measurement pipeline module responsible for sensor abstraction, data normalization, and acceptance criteria evaluation to support safety-critical decision-making across the system.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Raw sensor data ingestion from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems; pluggable sensor adapters normalize heterogeneous inputs.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Acceptance criteria evaluation results and safety-relevant measurement state communicated to downstream safety decision logic.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Configuration of measurement pipeline acceptance thresholds and sensor selection criteria from mission planning.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Optional: remote sensor data or calibration updates in teleoperated scenarios; measurement pipeline state telemetry for remote monitoring.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: measurement pipeline, normalization, and acceptance criteria evaluation are inherently safety-critical functions. Sensor abstraction layer naturally resides here to decouple safety logic from hardware specifics. Pluggable architecture aligns with Safety Services' need for flexible, composable safety mechanisms. Minor diffusion: Telemetry ingestion requires Vehicle/Teleoperation coordination, but normalization and evaluation remain fully contained.

Poor fit: Vehicle is primarily a hardware/actuation platform. Measurement pipeline logic (normalization, acceptance criteria) would diffuse into safety decision-making, violating separation of concerns. Sensor abstraction could reside here, but decoupling intent is lost. Acceptance criteria evaluation belongs in safety domain, not vehicle domain.

Poor fit: Teleoperation is a command/control interface layer. Measurement pipeline is orthogonal to remote operation concerns. Placing it here would couple safety-critical measurement logic to operator interface, creating tight coupling and diffusion across Vehicle and Safety Services.

Poor fit: NGHE Solution is a top-level system container. Placing measurement pipeline here would be overly broad and would not establish clear ownership or interface boundaries. Measurement pipeline is a specific, cohesive subsystem that should be owned by a domain-specific module (Safety Services), not the solution envelope.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The decoupled measurement pipeline (DP5) is fundamentally a safety-critical data processing function. Its three components—pluggable sensor abstraction, normalization layer, and acceptance criteria evaluation—are all safety-domain concerns. Safety Services is the natural owner because: (1) acceptance criteria evaluation directly supports safety decision-making; (2) sensor abstraction decouples safety logic from hardware variability, a core safety principle; (3) normalization ensures consistent, validated data for downstream safety consumers. The module boundary is tight, with clear ingress (Telemetry, Tasking) and egress (SafetyAlert, Network Link) interfaces.
containment_risks: Sensor abstraction may require bidirectional communication with Vehicle to discover available sensors and their capabilities; this could introduce coupling if Vehicle sensor registry is not well-defined.

Normalization rules and acceptance thresholds may need to be tuned per vehicle type or mission; if these are hardcoded in Safety Services, diffusion occurs when new vehicle types are added. Mitigation: parameterize via Tasking interface.

Real-time performance constraints: if measurement pipeline must meet strict latency bounds, Safety Services ownership is still correct, but implementation may require co-location with Vehicle hardware (physical proximity), not logical diffusion.
Teleoperation scenarios may require operator-driven override of acceptance criteria; this creates a secondary input path that could bypass Safety Services logic if not carefully architected.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP5 (decoupled measurement pipeline with pluggable sensor abstraction, normalization layer, and acceptance criteria evaluation) directly and sufficiently addresses FR (enable measurement and reporting of material throughput in volumetric or mass units per unit time). The three DP components—sensor abstraction, normalization, and acceptance criteria evaluation—collectively provide the logical mechanisms to ingest heterogeneous sensor data, standardize it, and validate it against throughput targets. The pluggable architecture ensures extensibility to new sensor types without redesign. DP is solution-agnostic and domain-appropriate.

diffusion_risks: Sensor abstraction may require bidirectional discovery protocol with Vehicle module to enumerate available sensors and their metadata (units, calibration, sampling rate). If Vehicle sensor registry is not formally defined and versioned, coupling risk increases. Mitigation: establish Vehicle→Safety Services sensor capability interface as a read-only contract.

Normalization rules and acceptance thresholds are parameterized via Tasking interface, but if mission-specific tuning requires Vehicle-type-specific coefficients (e.g., different throughput targets per vehicle model), Safety Services must not hardcode these. Risk: diffusion occurs if new vehicle types force code changes in Safety Services. Mitigation: externalize all vehicle-type-dependent parameters to Tasking configuration schema.

Real-time latency constraints: if measurement pipeline must meet sub-100ms evaluation cycles to support safety decisions, Safety Services logical ownership remains correct, but physical co-location or shared-memory architecture with Vehicle may be required. This is an implementation detail (IM realization), not a logical diffusion, but must be tracked in deployment architecture.

Teleoperation override scenarios: if operator can remotely override acceptance thresholds or sensor selection during teleoperated missions, this creates a secondary input path (Network Link inbound). Risk: if override logic is not centralized in Safety Services, safety decision-making diffuses across Teleoperation and Safety Services. Mitigation: all threshold overrides must flow through Tasking or a dedicated SafetyOverride interface owned by Safety Services.

Sensor data quality assessment (outlier detection, sensor fault diagnosis) is implicit in 'normalization layer' but not explicitly named. If this logic is scattered across Vehicle (sensor-level validation) and Safety Services (pipeline-level validation), diffusion occurs. Mitigation: clarify whether normalization includes fault detection or if Vehicle is responsible for sensor health; document boundary.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Safety Services is the correct IM owner with high containment score (0.92). The module boundary is tight and well-defined: ingress via Telemetry (raw sensor data) and Tasking (configuration); egress via SafetyAlert (evaluation results) and Network Link (telemetry/state). The three DP components are cohesive safety-domain concerns. Vehicle and Teleoperation are correctly excluded (diffusion scores 0.55 and 0.60 respectively). Minor diffusion risk exists at the Telemetry ingestion boundary (Vehicle coordination), but this is a data flow dependency, not a logical ownership ambiguity. Recommended: formalize the Telemetry interface contract to specify sensor data schema, units, and metadata; ensure Vehicle provides sensor discovery as a read-only service.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The acceptance criterion 'reported productivity ≥ productivity_target over evaluation period' is directly measurable via the measurement pipeline's output (SafetyAlert interface). Verification requires: (1) instrumentation of Safety Services to log all measurement pipeline outputs (sensor readings, normalized values, acceptance criteria results); (2) comparison of reported throughput against ground-truth productivity targets during representative operations; (3) validation that normalization and acceptance criteria logic correctly transform raw sensor data into productivity metrics. Feasibility is strong because Safety Services owns the entire measurement chain and can emit audit-trail telemetry. Worksite safety plan and operational policies are external constraints that inform acceptance threshold configuration (via Tasking interface), not direct verification targets of this IM.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. FR is allocated to DP5, which is realized in Safety Services IM. The allocation is unambiguous: Safety Services is the single owner of measurement pipeline logic. Interfaces are well-defined (Telemetry in, SafetyAlert out, Tasking config, Network Link optional). No competing allocations or shared ownership. Clarity is high. Recommendation: document the measurement pipeline's internal architecture (sensor adapter registry, normalization rule engine, acceptance criteria evaluator) as sub-components within Safety Services to enable future traceability and testing.

recommended_revisions: Formalize the Telemetry interface contract: specify sensor data schema (units, precision, sampling rate, metadata), error handling (missing data, out-of-range values), and latency bounds. This clarifies the Vehicle→Safety Services boundary and reduces coupling risk.

Explicitly define the scope of 'normalization layer': does it include sensor fault detection, outlier filtering, unit conversion, or all three? Document which responsibilities belong to Vehicle (sensor-level validation) vs. Safety Services (pipeline-level validation) to prevent diffusion.

Parameterize all acceptance thresholds and vehicle-type-specific coefficients via the Tasking interface. Ensure Safety Services does not hardcode productivity targets or sensor-selection logic. This prevents diffusion when new vehicle types or mission profiles are added.

If Teleoperation scenarios require operator override of acceptance criteria, establish a formal SafetyOverride input interface (or extend Tasking) owned by Safety Services. Document the override authorization policy and audit trail requirements.

Add a sub-component diagram within Safety Services showing: (1) sensor adapter registry (pluggable abstraction), (2) normalization rule engine, (3) acceptance criteria evaluator, and (4) state/telemetry aggregator. This clarifies internal cohesion and supports future testing and maintenance.

Specify real-time performance requirements (latency, throughput) for the measurement pipeline and validate that Safety Services IM can meet them. If co-location with Vehicle hardware is required, document this as a deployment constraint, not a logical ownership change.

Define the measurement pipeline's behavior under sensor failure or data unavailability: does it degrade gracefully, halt safety decisions, or escalate to operator? Document this in SafetyAlert interface specification.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence. However, the gate results expose critical sub-system concerns that warrant decomposition before implementation: (1) the 'normalization layer' is underspecified—it conflates unit conversion, outlier filtering, and sensor fault detection without clear ownership boundaries; (2) sensor discovery and metadata exchange with Vehicle requires a formal contract to prevent coupling drift; (3) acceptance criteria parameterization must be externalized to prevent vehicle-type-specific diffusion; (4) real-time latency constraints and teleoperation override scenarios introduce secondary functional concerns not yet explicitly modeled as sub-FRs. Given high safety criticality, decomposing these concerns into explicit sub-FRs ensures that each logical responsibility is independently validated, traced, and owned. Stopping here risks deferring critical architectural decisions to implementation, where they become costly to correct.
next_level_sub_frs: FR5.1: Ingest heterogeneous sensor data (raw measurements with metadata) from Vehicle via standardized Telemetry interface contract, handling missing data, out-of-range values, and latency variability.
FR5.2: Normalize sensor data to common units and precision (unit conversion, scaling, precision alignment) and detect sensor faults or outliers to ensure data quality before acceptance evaluation.
FR5.3: Evaluate normalized throughput measurements against parameterized acceptance criteria (productivity targets, thresholds, evaluation windows) and emit pass/fail verdicts with audit trail.

FR5.4: Manage acceptance threshold and vehicle-type-specific coefficient parameters via external configuration (Tasking interface) to support multi-vehicle and multi-mission scenarios without code changes.
FR5.5: Support operator override of acceptance criteria during teleoperated missions via formal SafetyOverride input interface, with authorization policy and audit logging.
FR5.6: Ensure measurement pipeline evaluation latency meets real-time constraints (sub-100ms cycles) to support timely safety decision-making without degrading measurement accuracy.

revise.why: DP5 is logically sound but insufficiently detailed to guide implementation without ambiguity. Decomposition will refine DP5 into six sub-DPs, each addressing a specific sub-FR. This ensures that: (1) sensor abstraction and discovery are explicitly modeled (DP5.1); (2) normalization and fault detection responsibilities are separated and owned (DP5.2); (3) acceptance criteria evaluation is decoupled from parameterization (DP5.3 and DP5.4); (4) teleoperation override logic is formalized (DP5.5); (5) real-time performance is explicitly addressed (DP5.6). Revising DP at this stage prevents downstream implementation ambiguity and reduces the risk of diffusion across module boundaries.

assumptions: Vehicle module provides a read-only sensor discovery service (registry of available sensors, units, calibration metadata, sampling rates) accessible via a formal interface contract.
Tasking interface supports parameterized configuration of acceptance thresholds, vehicle-type-specific coefficients, and evaluation windows without requiring Safety Services code changes.

Teleoperation override scenarios (if present) are infrequent and do not dominate the measurement pipeline's primary path; override logic can be centralized in Safety Services without performance penalty.

Real-time latency constraints (sub-100ms evaluation cycles) are achievable via in-process or shared-memory architecture; no distributed consensus or external service calls are required in the critical path.
Sensor fault detection and outlier filtering are Safety Services responsibilities; Vehicle is responsible only for sensor-level validation (e.g., range checks at the sensor driver level).
Measurement pipeline audit trail (logging of all sensor readings, normalized values, acceptance verdicts) is required for safety compliance and post-incident analysis.
open_risks: Telemetry interface contract is not yet formalized: sensor data schema, error handling, latency bounds, and metadata format must be jointly defined by Vehicle and Safety Services to prevent coupling drift.
Normalization layer scope is ambiguous: unit conversion, outlier filtering, and sensor fault detection are conflated. Clarification required before DP5.2 is detailed.
Vehicle-type-specific coefficients may proliferate if Tasking configuration schema is not carefully designed; risk of diffusion if new vehicle types force Safety Services code changes.
Teleoperation override authorization policy is not defined: unclear who can override, under what conditions, and with what audit trail. This may require a formal SafetyOverride interface.
Real-time latency constraints are mentioned but not quantified in the original FR. Sub-100ms assumption may be incorrect; verification required.
Sensor failure behavior is underspecified: does the pipeline degrade gracefully, halt, or escalate? This affects safety decision-making and must be explicitly modeled.
Measurement pipeline's internal state management (e.g., buffering, windowing for evaluation periods) is not yet addressed; may require sub-DP for state aggregation.
evidence_needed: Formal Telemetry interface specification (schema, error codes, latency SLA, metadata format) co-authored by Vehicle and Safety Services teams.
Clarification document: normalization layer responsibilities (unit conversion, outlier filtering, fault detection) with explicit ownership boundaries (Vehicle vs. Safety Services).

Tasking configuration schema design document showing parameterization of acceptance thresholds, vehicle-type coefficients, and evaluation windows; validation that Safety Services does not hardcode vehicle-specific logic.
Real-time performance requirements specification: latency bounds, throughput targets, and deployment architecture (in-process vs. distributed) for the measurement pipeline.
Teleoperation override policy document (if applicable): authorization rules, audit trail format, and integration with SafetyAlert interface.
Sensor failure mode analysis: behavior of measurement pipeline under sensor unavailability, fault detection, and graceful degradation strategies.
Sub-component architecture diagram for Safety Services showing sensor adapter registry, normalization rule engine, acceptance criteria evaluator, and state aggregator with internal interfaces.

Test plan for measurement pipeline: unit tests for normalization logic, integration tests for sensor abstraction, and end-to-end tests for acceptance criteria evaluation with representative sensor data.
